planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions or container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; common polypody naturally grows on rock outcrops, dry stone walls, and tree bases — it thrives in conditions with excellent drainage and bright indirect light where other ferns fail.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich to gritty soil in partial to full shade; common polypody is among the most distinctive of all hardy ferns — its fronds are once-pinnate, with rounded, smooth-edged lobes rather than the further-divided, delicate-looking fronds of most ferns, giving each frond a simple, bold quality like a large green comb. The fronds are evergreen, remaining green through winter and refreshing only in spring. Large, round, golden spore dots (sori) on the underside of the fronds are ornamental features in themselves. The plant grows naturally on dry, shaded rock outcrops, stone walls, and epiphytically on the bark of trees — it is specifically adapted to good drainage and does not tolerate soggy soil. Excellent for shaded rock gardens, dry stone walls, and crevice gardens. The rhizomes have a pleasant, licorice-like scent when cut. A charming, low-maintenance fern for difficult dry-shade situations where most ferns fail.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions or container-grown specimens in consistently moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full shade in spring; oak fern requires cool, consistently moist conditions and is not suited to hot or dry sites — it thrives under conifers and in moist ravines in cool northern and mountain climates.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full shade; oak fern is one of the most exquisitely delicate and beautiful of all small shade ferns — each frond consists of a nearly horizontal, bright apple-green, triangular blade divided into three nearly equal, spreading lobes, held on a slender black stalk so that the fronds appear to float in mid-air at ankle height like a constellation of tiny green triangles. The effect of a colony of oak fern spreading across the dark forest floor is luminous and ethereal. It spreads slowly by underground rhizomes to form a low, delicate, weed-suppressing groundcover under large conifers and northern hardwoods in moist, cool gardens. It requires consistently moist, cool conditions and is best suited to zones 2–7 in cool-summer climates; it struggles in hot, humid summers south of zone 6. An essential fern for naturalistic cool-climate shade garden compositions with mosses, trilliums, and Canada mayflower.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; hay-scented fern spreads aggressively by rhizomes and is best used where large-scale coverage is desired — it is an excellent erosion-control fern for slopes and disturbed woodland edges but can be invasive in smaller gardens.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; hay-scented fern is a highly adaptable native fern of eastern North American forests and disturbed woodland edges that spreads vigorously by rhizomes to form large, dense, arching colonies of bright-green, finely cut, lacy fronds — the fronds exude a sweet, fresh hay-like fragrance when crushed or bruised (the characteristic scent for which it is named). It is one of the most drought-tolerant of all shade ferns, thriving in conditions that would stress or kill more delicate ferns, and it tolerates more sun than most ferns. Turn golden-yellow in fall. Best used where large-scale fern groundcover is needed — on shaded slopes, in natural areas under tree canopies, or as a woodland-edge erosion-control planting. In smaller ornamental gardens its spreading habit can become problematic; use barriers or plant in large naturalized areas. A critical deer-resistant native.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in consistently moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full shade in spring; long beech fern requires cool, moist, acidic conditions and is most successful in zones 2–6 in cool, shaded ravines, stream banks, and moist forest understories.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full shade near streams or in cool, moist ravines; long beech fern (northern beech fern) is a delicate native fern of cool, moist, acidic northern forests, recognizable by the characteristic shape of its fronds — triangular in overall outline with the lowest pair of pinnae (leaflets) angled sharply downward and forward, giving each frond a distinctive winged appearance as if the bottom pair of segments were stretched down toward the ground. The fronds are soft-textured, softly hairy, and bright green with a fresh, cool quality. Plants spread slowly by underground rhizomes to form loose colonies on moist, shaded stream banks and in ravines. Requires consistently moist, cool, acidic conditions similar to its forest habitat and is not suited to drier or warmer gardens south of zone 6. Excellent for naturalizing in cool, moist, shaded stream-side gardens alongside ostrich ferns, interrupted ferns, and blue cohosh.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; eared lady fern tolerates more warmth and humidity than Japanese painted fern (A. niponicum) and is an excellent choice for warmer temperate shade gardens in zones 5–8.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; eared lady fern is a Japanese native in the same genus as the celebrated Japanese painted fern (Athyrium niponicum 'Pictum') but with distinct and beautiful ornamental qualities — the bipinnate fronds are a rich, lustrous, dark-green color, and the stipes (leaf stalks) and rachises (midribs) are a striking deep wine-red to burgundy color that provides vivid two-tone contrast throughout the growing season. The reddish rachis color persists through the entire frond surface, creating a subtle but attractive color effect visible throughout the growing season. It forms a tidy, non-spreading clump unlike some invasive ferns and is a reliable performer in warmer shade gardens where the Japanese painted fern sometimes struggles. Excellent combined with hostas, Japanese forest grass, and astilbes in classic shade garden compositions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
